title: Conditional Transitions
concept: Routing execution paths dynamically based on node output flags (e.g. validation pass/fail).

exercises.prompt: What do we use to hold shared information across different nodes in a graph-based orchestration?
exercises.explain: We use a global state to share information across different nodes, enabling coordinated execution.

cards.title: Conditional Transitions
cards.body: Conditional transitions allow nodes to change their execution paths based on the outcome of a previous action, like a traffic light that changes based on oncoming traffic.

cards.title: What are Conditional Transitions?
cards.body: Conditional transitions are changes in the flow of execution depending on certain conditions or flags. For example, if validation passes, move to Step A; if it fails, move to Step B.

cards.title: Traffic Light Analogy
cards.body: Think of this like a traffic light. It changes to green for go when conditions are safe and red for stop when they're not. Similarly, a node's path can change based on its conditions.

cards.title: Example of Conditional Transitions
cards.body: Imagine a registration process. If a user completes validation successfully, they proceed to the welcome page. If not, they see an error message instead.

cards.title: Myth: Conditional Transitions are Complicated
cards.body: Some believe conditional transitions are too complex, but they're just simple decision points that guide the flow based on outputs.

cards.title: Visualizing Conditional Transitions
cards.body: Conditional transitions can be visualized like a fork in the road, where the direction taken depends on the sign seen before.

exercises.prompt: Which is a key feature of conditional transitions?

options.text: They adapt the path based on output flags.
They simplify all outputs into one result.

exercises.explain: Conditional transitions adapt the execution path based on the output flags, allowing dynamic routing instead of a fixed path.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Conditional transitions always follow the same path regardless of output flags.
exercises.explain: False. Conditional transitions allow routing to change based on the outputs of previous nodes.

exercises.prompt: In a conditional transition, the execution path changes based on the outcome of a ______.
exercises.sentence: In a conditional transition, the execution path changes based on the outcome of a flag.
exercises.explain: The path changes based on the outcome of a flag, which indicates whether a condition is met or not.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best describes conditional transitions in a graph?

options.text: They can only exist in linear paths.
They guide transitions based on outcomes or flags.
They eliminate all decisions in the graph.

exercises.explain: Conditional transitions guide the flow based on specific outcomes, allowing for a dynamic execution process.

exercises.prompt: How do conditional transitions affect the overall execution flow in graph-based orchestration?

options.text: They cause the execution process to become purely linear.
They enable dynamic changes to the path based on conditions.
They remove all flexibility from the orchestration.
They rely on static outputs from previous nodes.

exercises.explain: Conditional transitions enhance flexibility by allowing the execution path to dynamically change based on conditions or flags, improving responsiveness in the orchestration.
